original_abstract: Between 1951 and 1991, approximately 17 different processes were developed to recover uranium from wet phosphoric acid (WPA). However, the industrial viability of these methods has historically fluctuated alongside the volatile global uranium market, leading to the decommissioning of all such recovery plants by the late 1990s. Today, the extraction of uranium from WPA has regained significant interest, driven by rising global demand and the expansion of nuclear energy programs in developing nations. This renewed relevance provides a strong impetus to re-examine legacy technologies, seek process improvements, and explore entirely new chemical approaches. This paper provides a comprehensive review of historical extraction processes and the underlying physicochemistry that governed their performance. Furthermore, we report and discuss recent advances in the development of novel extraction systems designed to enhance efficiency and selectivity in modern phosphoric acid media.
humanized_abstract: The estimated number of uranium processes developed to extract uranium from wet phosphoric acid (WPA) over the 40 years from 1951 to 1991 has been estimated as seventeen. These processes were sensitive to the uranium market and following fluctuations were almost without exception backed down whenever uranium prices fell. All WPA uranium extraction plants were shutdown by the end of the 1990's. However, with an increasing world uranium requirement, anticipated growth of the nuclear power program in emerging countries and a re-evaluation of existing technology, a review of potential applications of historic solutions, in addition to possible improvements, with the possible development of new and alternative technologies to extract uranium efficiently and selectively in present day WPA hydrometallurgy conditions is pertinent. This article makes a retrospective evaluation of the various historic extraction processes together with the associated underlying physicochemistry, reviews present hydrometallurgical developments including recent studies on new extraction process developments applicable in present day phosphoric acid containing uranium solutions.
